content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacrame nto, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
William Sperling 
COO/General Counsel 
LAUP 
888 S. Figueroa Ave., Suite 800 
Los Angeles, CA 90045 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-025 
Dear Mr. Sperling: 
October 11, 2017 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of the Los Angeles Universal Preschool 
("LAUP") for advice regarding its status as a government agency under the provisions of the 
Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Is LAUP still a government agency for purposes of the Act even though it has undergone a 
transformation in its mission, funding system and governance? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, LAUP is still considered a government agency for purposes of the Act. 
FACTS 
You are the General Counsel for LAUP and are requesting advice regarding its status as an 
agency under the Act. LAUP was fonned in 2004 as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit, public benefit 
corporation, to administer a universal preschool system on behalf of and solely funded by First 5 
LA, a public agency for purposes of the Act. Around the time of its formation, counsel for First 5 
LA, Craig Steele, requested advice on whether the new corporation would be subject to the Act. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-025 
Page No. 2 
The Commission found that the new corporation would be subject to the Act. (Steele Advice Letter, 
No. A-04-072.) 
At the outset and until 2016, LAUP's mission was the distribution of Proposition IO tax 
revenues for children to attend new or expanded preschools. This work was done on behalf of First 
5 LA. The Steele Advice Letter was premised on LAUP's performance of a function delegated to it 
by First 5 LA to carry out the directives of a Proposition 10. During its twelve years of operation, 
LAUP was almost completely dependent on First 5 LA for financial support and virtually 
exclusively engaged in the implementation of Proposition 10. On June 30, 2016, LAUP's contract 
with First 5 LA to administer its preschool attendance program ended and so did its role of 
subsidizing preschool spaces. You stated that LAUP has now re-purposed itself. 
In lieu of funding preschool spaces under the universal preschool program, LAUP now 
provides support and training services to existing preschools. LAUP now receives funding from 
several government agencies and some outside sources and is no longer completely dependent on 
First 5 LA. Most of LAUP's large value contracts since the transition have been obtained by 
successfully completing an RFP or RFQ process and/or grant application. Prior to June 2016, 
LAUP received a $580M grant from First 5 LA to build a universal preschool system without 
having to compete because LAUP was created by First 5 LA's grant. LAUP now has to compete for 
smaller grants and contracts for work. 
LAUP is currently projected to be funded by the following entities in the following amounts 
for fiscal year 2017: 
California Department of Education via the LA County Office of Education - $17,100,000 
First 5 LA - $14,900,000 
LA County Office of Education Direct Services - $1,666,000 
Baldwin Park Unified School District - $291,000 
County of Orange Department of Education - $252,000 
First 5 Riverside - $ I 50,000 
Compton Unified School District - $146,000 
Kern County Department of Education - $100,000 
Kings County Department of Education - $74,000 
First 5 Santa Barbara - $42,000 
Various Private Foundations - $300,000 
During its first twelve years, the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors appointed five 
members to LAUP's Board and First 5 LA appointed an ex officio member. LAUP's board no 
longer includes any members appointed by a public agency. LAUP's board is now an elected board 
made up of people serving in their private capacities. LAUP's new contract with First 5 LA 
involves providing preschool quality improvements to enhance the preschool experience for 
children enrolled in early learning environments. LAUP no longer provides preschool subsidies to 
children. LAUP now provides quality improvement services to early education providers, a group 
that includes not only preschools but day care services as well. Specifically, LAUP conducts 
assessments of early education services using research proven assessment tools, and then uses those 
assessments to provide individualized coaching to early education providers to improve their 
quality. LAUP's services help relieve providers of administrative and compliance burdens which 


File No. A-17-025 
Page No. 3 
can detract from the provider's ability to maintain high quality services. LAUP's employees now 
serve as consultants to preschools to improve the quality of the experience they provide to 
children. The old mission was to be a preschool provider; the new mission is to be a quality 
improvement consultant. 
Since June of2016 LAUP has changed its bylaws. LAUP's meetings are no longer subject 
to the Brown Act. Additionally, LAUP has many competitors in the area of early education quality 
support including WestEd, Lakeshore, UCLA, ERS, Teachstone and many smaller companies and 
independent contractors who deliver quality support services. LAUP currently serves approximately 
241 privately owned providers along with 265 providers located on school district sites or 
community colleges. LAUP provides one-on-one coaching for early education teachers at times and 
dates and prices determined by the Los Angeles County Office of Education. LAUP provides 
preschool quality assessments in accordance with guidelines and procedures determined by First 5 
LA. 
ANALYSIS 
A "local government agency" is defined in the Act as "a county, city or district of any kind 
including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any department, 
division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing." (Section 82041.) 
The inquiry here is whether LAUP is still considered a local government agency under the 
Act. The Commission used a four-part factual test to distinguish governmental from non­
governmental entities in In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62. ln the Siegel opinion, the Commission 
was faced with the issue of whether the Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation, a nonprofit 
corporation, should be considered a local government entity. The Pico Rivera Water Development 
Corporation was a nonprofit corporation that was founded to acquire, maintain, and operate a water 
system. In analyzing whether the Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation was a governmental 
entity, the Commission set forth four criteria: 
(I) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a government agency; 
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of funds is, a 
government agency; 
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was formed is to provide 
services or undertake obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to perform and 
which, in fact, they traditionally have performed; and 
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions. 
Application of the Siegel Criteria. 
We apply the Siegel test to the detailed facts you have provided to determine whether LAUP 
should still be considered a ••local government agency" under the Act. 


File No. A-17-025 
Page No. 4 
1. Did the impetus for formation of the entity originate with a government entity? 
Generally, the first criterion of the Siegel test is met where an entity is created by statute or 
ordinance or by some official action of another governmental agency. (Maas Advice Letter, 
No. A-98-261; Moser Advice Letter, No. A-97-400.) In this case, the impetus for creating LAUP 
was a public agency, First 5 LA. First 5 LA, as a Proposition 10 commission, is considered a local 
government agency. (Tyrrell Advice Letter, No. 1-99-171.) 
2. Is the entity substantially funded by. or is its prima,y source offzmds. a government 
agency? 
Initially LAUP was funded entirely by a public agency, First 5 LA. Now LAUP is projected 
to be funded by several government agencies and some private entities. LAUP is projected to 
receive over $15 million from First 5 LA and over $17 million from the California Department of 
Education via the LA County Office of Education. LAUP is also projected to receive funds from 
other government agencies, but is projected to receive only $300,000 from private entities. While 
you argue in your letter that LAUP is no longer substantially funded by a government agency, we 
disagree. Almost all of the projected funding to LAUP listed above is coming from government 
agencies. It does not change the analysis under the Siegel test if more than one government agency 
is providing the funding. Instead, the test focuses on the total amount received from government 
agencies, not the number of agencies involved. (See van Hofien Advice Letter, No. A-156-141 and 
Olson Advice Letter, No. A-16-255.) 
You have stated that LAUP now receives funding from a variety of sources in a variety of 
ways, but most of its large value contracts have been obtained by successfully completing an RFP 
or RFQ process and/or grant application. We have taken the position in previous advice letters that 
if an entity receives most of its funding from government grant monies, it meets the second factor. 
(Matthews Advice Letter, No. A-99-276; Rasiah Advice Letter, No. A-01-020) Therefore, the 
second criterion of the Siegel test is met. 
3. Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed, to provide services or 
undertake obligations that public agencies are legally authorized to perform and which. in fact. 
they traditionally have performed? 
In the Siegel Opinion, this third criterion is a two-part inquiry that examines whether an 
entity performs a public function, and whether the service provided is one that is traditionally 
performed by public agencies. (Stark Advice Letter, No. A-03-015, Gale Advice Letter, No. A-16-
019.) 
When considering whether an entity serves a public function one factor we consider is the 
degree to which government actors control or are involved in the entities operations. Previously, 
LAUP's board consisted of members who were appointed by First 5 LA and other government 
entities, including the Board of Supervisors. Now, no outside entity has authority to appoint LAUP 
board members. LAUP's board is now made up of elected members. However, you noted that 
LAUP provides one-on-one coaching for early education teachers at times and dates and prices 
determined by the Los Angeles County Office of Education. Also, LAUP provides preschool 


File No. A-17-025 
Page No. 5 
quality assessments in accordance with guidelines and procedures determined by First 5 LA. This 
indicates that there is still involvement by government actors over LAUP's operations. 
Another factor is whether the service is one that is traditionally performed by public 
agencies. The initial purpose for creating LAUP was to run the universal preschool program and 
expend Proposition IO funds on behalf of First 5 LA for duties which First 5 LA would have 
otherwise performed. LAUP no longer runs the universal preschool program. Instead, LAUP now 
provides support and training services to existing preschools, most of them public schools but also 
some private schools. Specifically, LAUP conducts assessments of early education services using 
research proven assessment tools, and then uses those assessments to provide individualized 
coaching to early education providers to improve their quality. You have stated that LAUP's 
services help relieve providers of administrative and compliance burdens which can detract from 
the provider's ability to maintain high quality services. This suggests that LAUP performs services 
for schools that they, as public agencies, are required to perfonn. Thus, LAUP is performing 
services that are a public function and that public agencies have traditionally performed. 
4. ls the entity treated as a public entity by other statuto,yprovisions? 
You have stated that LAUP is not treated as a public agency by any statute or ordinance 
including the Brown Act, Bagley-Keene Open Meeting Act, Public Records Act, and the 
Administrative Procedures Act. Therefore, this factor is not met. "Nevertheless, we pointed out that 
the fourth criterion alone is not determinative as to whether an entity is a governmental agency." 
(Travis Advice Letter, No. A-96-053.) 
It is not necessary that all four of the criteria be satisfied for an entity to be considered a 
local government agency. (O'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity 
satisfy enough of the four criteria for its overall character to correspond to that of a local 
government agency. (Rasia/, Advice Letter, No. A-01-020.) Since three of the four Siegel criteria 
are met, LAUP would still be considered a local government agency for purposes of the Act. 
SKB:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Is LAUP still a government agency for purposes of the Act even though it has undergone a 
transformation in its mission, funding system and governance?
sections.conclusion: Yes, LAUP is still considered a government agency for purposes of the Act.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is LAUP still a government agency for purposes of the Act even though it has undergone a 
transformation in its mission, funding system and governance?

CONCLUSION: Yes, LAUP is still considered a government agency for purposes of the Act.
